result:
I want to write about a simple, profound thing I witnessed last week. It was late afternoon, and I was walking through a city park, one of those green lungs we carve out of concrete to remind ourselves we’re still animals. I was preoccupied, my mind a tangled knot of deadlines, unanswered emails, and a low-grade anxiety about the future—the usual static of modern life. My eyes were on the path, my pace brisk, efficient.

And then I stopped.

A massive oak tree, centuries old, presided over a clearing. Its bark was gnarled and furrowed like the skin of some wise, sleeping giant. At its base, a squirrel was frantically burying an acorn. It dug with furious, precise paws, placed its treasure, patted the earth down with an almost comical seriousness, then darted off to repeat the process. Above, in the canopy, a crow cawed, a harsh sound that somehow didn’t break the peace but deepened it. Sunlight, filtered through a billion leaves, fell in shifting, dappled coins of gold on the grass. A slight breeze carried the scent of damp earth and distant rain.

In that moment, the static in my head ceased. It wasn’t a dramatic revelation. No thunderclap, no voice from the heavens. It was a gentle, total immersion in the *present*. I was simply *there*. Not a thinker of thoughts, but a part of the scene—the observer and the observed, merged. I felt the age of the tree not as a fact, but as a palpable presence, a slow, patient hum of life completely unconcerned with my deadlines. The squirrel’s urgent work was not trivial; it was the entire, vital purpose of its being in that moment. The crow’s call was not an interruption, but the park’s own voice.

This is what we have lost, I thought. Or rather, what we have paved over. We have exiled ourselves from the present tense. Our natural state—the state of that squirrel, the tree, even the crow—is one of unselfconscious immersion. A bird doesn’t sing while worrying if its song is good enough or if it should be using its time more productively. It just sings. It *is* its song.

We, however, live almost exclusively in the abstract realms of memory and anticipation. We replay past slights and glories on a loop. We pre-live future anxieties and hopes, burning psychic energy on events that have not and may never happen. The present moment—the only moment that ever actually *exists*—has become a mere corridor we rush through to get to the next thing. We eat while watching TV, walk while listening to podcasts, sit with friends while scrolling through the lives of strangers. We treat the now as insufficient, needing constant augmentation from the digital elsewhere.

But in that park, the ‘elsewhere’ vanished. And with it, the anxiety that is its constant shadow. Anxiety is, at its root, a fear of future loss or a regret of past action. It cannot survive in a fully inhabited present. The tree is not anxious about next winter’s storms. It stands. It endures. It uses the sunlight offered today. There’s a deep intelligence in that, a wisdom far older than human philosophy.

This isn’t to advocate for a life of thoughtless instinct. Our capacity for reflection, foresight, and memory is our great gift and our terrible burden. It built cathedrals, wrote sonnets, and conceived of justice. But it is a tool that has become our master. We need to remember how to put it down. We need to practice being animals again.

It starts with the senses. The feel of cool water on your hands, not as a prelude to drying them, but as a complete experience—the temperature, the sound, the shimmer. The taste of a single strawberry, its sweetness exploding, its texture, the fact of it being a little sun-ripened globe of life. The sound of rain on a roof, not as background noise, but as a complex, rhythmic symphony. This is not “mindfulness” as a productivity hack or a stress-reduction technique marketed to us. This is the fundamental act of coming home to reality. It is the original prayer, the most basic act of gratitude: to fully receive a moment of your one, wild life.

I think of the artists who capture this. The poet who notices the way light slants on a kitchen table and makes it holy. The painter who sees the exact purple in a shadow. The musician who finds the note that sounds like heartbreak feels. They are translators of the present tense, reminding us to look, to listen.

There is also a profound ethics to this. When you are truly present with another person—not planning your response, not judging, just listening with your whole being—you offer them a rare sanctuary. You acknowledge their existence in its entirety. You see them. In a world of fragmented attention, that is an act of deep love. Similarly, being present to the natural world fosters not abstract environmental concern, but a felt kinship. You don’t protect a river you’ve never truly seen, heard, or touched.

My moment in the park passed. The squirrel vanished. The crow flew away. The knot of thoughts began to reform. But it was looser now. A thread of that peace remained. I walked on, but my pace was slower. I noticed the pattern of cracks in the pavement, the smile of a passing stranger, the weight of the air before the rain.

We are, all of us, homesick for the present. We long for that state of grace where we are not split—where our being and our doing are one. We chase it through vacations, hobbies, meditation apps, and substances. But it isn’t somewhere else. It’s right here. It’s in the next breath you take, fully aware of the cool influx of air. It’s in the next thing your eyes truly see, without naming or categorizing it, just witnessing its *is-ness*.

The oak tree doesn’t know it is a teacher. It simply is. It stands in the park, in the city, on the earth, offering its silent, ancient lesson to anyone who stops long enough to listen: *This is it. You are here. Now.* The treasure is not buried somewhere ahead. It is in the digging, the feeling of the earth, the scent of the rain, the sound of your own heart, beating in time with a world that is, for all its chaos, overwhelmingly, achingly alive in this very second. Our task is not to build a better future for all time, but to fully live this one, fleeting, magnificent moment. And then the next. And then the next.
